_purpose: Ground-truth, independently-verifiable facts backing the Pajaro River counterfactual model. Every field here traces to a live public source fetched or checked directly — this file exists so the model's claims can be audited, not just trusted.

watershed.name: Pajaro River
watershed.source: USGS Watershed Boundary Dataset, WBD MapServer layer 4 (8-digit HU / Subbasin), queried live 2026-07-20

gauge.name: PAJARO R A CHITTENDEN CA
gauge.note_on_area_discrepancy: 1186 sq mi (3071 km2) is the gauge's own contributing drainage area, measured at Chittenden — upstream of the full HUC8 basin (3368.62 km2), since Chittenden is not at the river mouth.
gauge.source: USGS NWIS site service (waterservices.usgs.gov/nwis/site), queried live 2026-07-20

march_2023_event.note: This is the observed discharge/stage at the Chittenden gauge, not measured at the levee breach itself (breach was near River Mile 10, downstream of Chittenden). It is the model's primary hindcast-calibration target.
march_2023_event.important_methodological_caveat: The Chittenden gauge's OWN peak stage (29.11 ft) did NOT exceed its own NWS flood stage (32 ft) during this event. The levee breach happened downstream at ~River Mile 10, where additional tributary inflow (e.g. Corralitos/Salsipuedes Creeks) and the specific levee's local condition — not the Chittenden gauge reading — determined the outcome. This means the model can rigorously validate against Chittenden (the only long-record real-time gauge on the mainstem) and speak to upstream-watershed peak-flow reduction, but should NOT claim to directly simulate the breach mechanics at the actual failure location — that is a distinct, localized levee-engineering question this model does not have the data or scope to answer.

levee_breach.location: Pajaro River left-bank levee, approximately River Mile 10, flooding the unincorporated community of Pajaro
levee_breach.source_note: Corroborated by USACE Sacramento District project page and multiple news reports; exact breach time not independently timestamped in sources found so far — flagged as a fact still needing a firmer citation before the pitch deck cites it precisely.

march_2023_event.source: USGS NWIS instantaneous values service (waterservices.usgs.gov/nwis/iv), queried live 2026-07-20, parameters 00060 (discharge, cfs) and 00065 (gage height, ft)

storm_precipitation.confidence: medium — a credible reported range from news/NOAA-adjacent coverage of this specific atmospheric river, not yet a single station-level precipitation time series. Good enough to characterize storm magnitude; not yet precise enough to be the model's literal rainfall input without picking a specific station or gridded product (e.g. PRISM) and justifying the choice.
storm_precipitation.next_step: Pull an actual station time series (NOAA CDO needs an API token; CNRFC or a specific ASOS/COOP station near Corralitos/Watsonville would give an hourly curve instead of just a storm-total range) before this becomes the model's literal input rather than a calibration sanity-check.

levee_protection_level.characterization: Widely reported as one of the lowest levels of protection of any federal flood control levee system in California

original_1998_usace_design_capacity_cfs.source: 1998 USACE study, cited via USACE San Francisco District GRR (General Reevaluation Report) pre-conference materials, Nov 2000
original_1998_usace_design_capacity_cfs.important_note: The 2023 peak at Chittenden (13,200 cfs) was BELOW this original 1998 design capacity, yet the levee still breached downstream. This means the '8-year protection' figure describes the levee's DEGRADED/effective capacity by 2023 (sedimentation, vegetation, deferred maintenance), not the original design capacity — the two numbers are not directly comparable and the model must not conflate them.

current_project.name: Pajaro Regional Flood Risk Management Project
current_project.target_protection: Mix of 100-year and 25-year flood protection for Watsonville, Pajaro, and surrounding agricultural areas

levee_protection_level.environmental_justice_note: Multiple sources report that levee funding/reconstruction was delayed for decades in part because low-income and agricultural communities were not prioritized in cost-benefit analyses — this is documented public-record reporting, not this project's own characterization.
levee_protection_level.status: Have the original 1998 design capacity (19,000-22,000 cfs) but NOT a specific effective/degraded capacity number matching the '8-year' figure — that would need a primary USACE risk-assessment document, not yet located.
levee_protection_level.confidence: medium — sourced from news/advocacy reporting and secondary citation of a USACE report, not yet cross-checked against the primary document itself.

land_use_and_infiltration_baseline.source: USGS Pajaro Valley Hydrologic Model (PVHM) summary via pvwater.org — 'since about 1993, growers in the Pajaro Valley have shifted to more water intensive crops, such as strawberries, bushberries, and vegetable row crops'
land_use_and_infiltration_baseline.note: PVHM is a real, peer-reviewed USGS MODFLOW groundwater model (1964-2009) for this valley — a credibility anchor to cite ('a rigorous USGS groundwater model already exists for this valley; this project is a complementary surface-infiltration/flood tool, not a competitor') — but it does not itself publish a simple Ksat/soil-infiltration number usable directly as a model input.

counterfactual_infiltration_assumption.approach: Rather than inventing a Pajaro-specific improvement percentage, borrow the strongest INDEPENDENTLY-MEASURED comparison already vetted on this site: Singing Frogs Farm (Sebastopol, CA) measured ~7x greater water infiltration versus a neighboring tilled organic farm growing the same crop.
counterfactual_infiltration_assumption.source: regen-ag-case-studies.html on this site, itself sourced to Singing Frogs Farm's own published on-farm comparison
counterfactual_infiltration_assumption.honesty_requirement: Must be presented explicitly as an ANALOGY/scenario assumption applied to Pajaro (i.e. 'if upstream ag land achieved an infiltration improvement of the magnitude independently measured at Singing Frogs Farm elsewhere in California') — not as a Pajaro-specific soil study finding. This is the single most important place in the whole model where overclaiming would be easy and must be avoided.

land_use_and_infiltration_baseline.still_needed_for_full_rigor: An actual NRCS SSURGO/gSSURGO soil-series pull for the HUC8 to replace the current illustrative Ksat values with soil-series-specific ones — flagged as a Should-have, not blocking the first defensible version.
